questions.question: Which war was ended by the Treaty of Appomattox Court House in 1865, where Confederate General Robert E. Lee surrendered?
questions.explanation: General Robert E. Lee surrendered to Union General Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox Court House, Virginia, on April 9, 1865, effectively ending the American Civil War (1861-1865). The war was fought between the Northern states (Union) and Southern states (Confederacy) primarily over slavery and states' rights. About 620,000 soldiers died — more than in any other American conflict. Lincoln was assassinated just five days after Lee's surrender.
questions.memory_hook: Civil War ended April 9, 1865. Lee surrendered to Grant. Lincoln assassinated 5 days later. The war that ended slavery cost more American lives than any other — and the peace that followed, Reconstruction, was imperfect and short-lived.

questions.question: The Warsaw Pact, a military alliance of communist nations, was formed in 1955 as a response to which Western alliance?
questions.explanation: The Warsaw Pact (formally the Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance) was formed in May 1955 by the Soviet Union and seven Eastern European communist states, largely in response to West Germany joining NATO. It bound its members to mutual defense and Soviet military leadership. The Pact effectively kept Eastern Europe under Soviet military dominance until it dissolved in 1991 after the fall of communism.
questions.memory_hook: Warsaw Pact = communist answer to NATO. Formed 1955. Soviet Union + 7 Eastern European satellites. Collapsed in 1991 with the USSR. Most former Warsaw Pact countries have since joined NATO — the ultimate Cold War reversal.

questions.question: Which African country was formerly known as Abyssinia?
questions.explanation: Ethiopia was known as Abyssinia for centuries before gradually transitioning to its current name. It is one of the oldest nations in the world, with a history dating back over 3,000 years. Ethiopia is the only African country that was never colonized (it briefly endured Italian occupation 1936-1941 but was never fully subjugated). Its ancient Christian church, the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, traces its origins to the 4th century AD.
questions.memory_hook: Ethiopia = formerly Abyssinia. The only African country never truly colonized. Ancient, proud history — the Ark of the Covenant is said to be kept in a church in Axum. The only African nation to defeat a European colonial army at the Battle of Adwa in 1896.

questions.question: The Battle of Hastings in 1066 resulted in the Norman conquest of which country?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Hastings on October 14, 1066, saw William the Conqueror of Normandy defeat King Harold II of England. Harold was killed — reportedly shot in the eye by an arrow — and William became King William I of England. The conquest dramatically changed English culture, language (French became the language of the court), law, and society. About a third of English vocabulary comes from French as a result.
questions.memory_hook: Hastings 1066 = Normans conquer England. Harold killed (arrow in the eye, supposedly). William the Conqueror becomes king. French vocabulary floods English — that's why English has so many French-origin words for aristocracy, cuisine, law, and war.

questions.question: At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, which sprinter set a world record in the 100m, finishing in 9.69 seconds?
questions.explanation: Usain Bolt of Jamaica set the world record at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, running 100 meters in 9.69 seconds — while slowing down in the final meters to celebrate. He lowered his own record to 9.58 at the 2009 World Championships. Bolt holds world records in both the 100m and 200m and won three consecutive Olympic 100m gold medals (2008, 2012, 2016).
questions.memory_hook: Bolt, 9.69, Beijing 2008 — while celebrating. He was slowing down at the finish and still broke the world record. At 9.58 (his actual record) his stride length was 2.44 meters. He covered the 100m in 41 strides. Most humans need 55-60.

questions.question: In which sport do athletes compete for the Ashes trophy?
questions.explanation: The Ashes is a Test cricket series played between England and Australia, with the trophy being a small urn containing the ashes of a burnt cricket bail. The series began in 1882 after Australia beat England in England for the first time. A mock obituary declared that English cricket had 'died' and its 'ashes' were taken to Australia. The two countries have competed for the symbolic urn ever since.
questions.memory_hook: The Ashes = England vs Australia cricket. The urn contains actual ashes of a cricket bail. Started in 1882 as a joke in a newspaper — and became one of sport's oldest and most intense rivalries. The physical urn barely leaves the Lord's Cricket Ground museum.

questions.question: What does 'UCI' stand for in professional cycling?
questions.explanation: The UCI (Union Cycliste Internationale) is the world governing body for cycling, founded in 1900 and based in Aigle, Switzerland. It oversees all competitive cycling including road, track, mountain bike, BMX, and cyclo-cross. The UCI governs the Tour de France (though the ASO organizes it), establishes technical regulations, and runs the UCI WorldTour ranking system.
questions.memory_hook: UCI = Union Cycliste Internationale. French-origin name because cycling's major events (Tour de France, Paris-Roubaix, Liege-Bastogne-Liege) all started in France or Belgium. Founded 1900 — almost as old as the modern Olympics.

questions.question: Which DreamWorks animated film, released in 2001, follows a green ogre who goes on a quest to rescue Princess Fiona from a dragon-guarded castle, accompanied by a fast-talking Donkey?
questions.explanation: Shrek (2001), directed by Andrew Adamson and Vicky Jenson, was DreamWorks Animation's breakthrough hit. It won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Animated Feature. Mike Myers voiced Shrek, Eddie Murphy voiced Donkey, and Cameron Diaz voiced Princess Fiona. Its irreverent humor and fairy-tale parody spawned three sequels.
questions.memory_hook: Shrek 2001: DreamWorks, won first Oscar for Best Animated Feature. Ogre + Donkey.

questions.question: Which 1999 film directed by the Wachowski siblings featured Keanu Reeves discovering that reality is a simulation?
questions.explanation: The Matrix (1999) starred Keanu Reeves as Neo, a hacker who discovers that human reality is a computer simulation created by machines. The film pioneered 'bullet time' special effects and became a cultural phenomenon. It won four Academy Awards and grossed $463 million. Its philosophical questions about reality and free will have made it a staple of philosophy courses.
questions.memory_hook: The Matrix, 1999. Red pill or blue pill. Bullet time effects. Neo dodges bullets in slow motion — a technique copied in hundreds of films since. The Wachowskis pitched it by hiring martial artists to train all the principal actors for six months before filming.

questions.question: Which musician has the most number-one hits on the Billboard Hot 100, with 42 as of 2024?
questions.explanation: Mariah Carey holds the record for the most number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100, with 19 number-one songs as her sole or co-credited artist (42 total if counting her songwriting contributions). Her vocal range of five octaves is legendary. 'All I Want for Christmas Is You' (1994) has become a perennial holiday chart-topper, re-entering the top 10 annually for 30 years.
questions.memory_hook: Mariah Carey = most #1s on the Billboard Hot 100. 5-octave vocal range. 'All I Want for Christmas Is You' has re-entered the top 10 every Christmas for 30 years — the most successful holiday song of all time.

questions.question: Which country is home to Angkor Wat, the world's largest religious monument?
questions.explanation: Angkor Wat is a temple complex in Siem Reap, Cambodia, built in the 12th century by the Khmer Empire under King Suryavarman II. At roughly 163 hectares, it is the world's largest religious monument. Originally built as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu, it was later converted to Buddhism. The image of Angkor Wat appears on the Cambodian national flag.
questions.memory_hook: Angkor Wat = Cambodia. World's largest religious monument. Built in the 12th century — older than most European cathedrals. It appears on Cambodia's flag, which makes it the only flag to feature a building. Rediscovered by Europeans in 1860 — locals always knew it was there.

questions.question: What is the name of the Earth's natural satellite?
questions.explanation: Earth has one natural satellite, called the Moon. It is the fifth-largest moon in the solar system and the largest relative to the size of its host planet. The Moon stabilizes Earth's axial tilt (preventing extreme climate shifts), causes ocean tides, and has influenced calendars and navigation throughout human history. Twelve people have walked on the Moon — all American, all between 1969 and 1972.
questions.memory_hook: Earth has one moon — just 'the Moon.' 12 people have walked on it, all between 1969-1972. The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth at 3.8 cm per year. In the far future, it will stop making total solar eclipses because it'll be too far away.
